ships.positionNote: Off West Africa — heading for Cape of Good Hope

southKorea.note: Export cap in force since March 13

hormuz.note: CLOSED DE FACTO — US blockade ordered Apr 13 1400GMT; 15 ships/day Iranian cap maintained; VLCC U-turns confirmed; Trump blockade threat — Apr 13 2026

babMandeb.note: THREATENED — Iran parliament speaker signals expansion of maritime blockade beyond Hormuz; ~30 Houthi tankers near Saudi Yanbu within strike range; Bab el-Mandeb already high-risk from prior Houthi attacks 2026-04-05

suez.note: All traffic around Cape of Good Hope

household.note: Based on ABS 2023-24 Monthly Household Spending Indicator + 2022-23 HES. Crisis surcharge: 3-5% applied to transport, food, utilities, goods, health (not rent/mortgage). Includes direct fuel cost + indirect surcharge pass-through.

tiers.name: Pensioner / Concession
tiers.notes: Pension $1,116/mo. Debt: $4,200. Liquid savings $2,500 — constrained by age pension assets test; 5yr cost erosion has depleted buffer. Crisis surcharge +$325/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$1,058/mo. Crisis total: -$1,383/mo. Depletes W9.7 — savings exhausted by war start.
tiers.savingsSource: RBA 2024 Bulletin: many lower-income households hold liquid assets less than their fortnightly income. 65-74 median cash $15,829 (Westpac 2025) but pensioners face assets test constraints and 5yr cost-of-living erosion. Estimate: 2–3 weeks accessible cash buffer.

tiers.name: Low Income Worker
tiers.notes: Min wage $2,128/mo. Debt: $27,500. Liquid savings $1,200 — near-zero buffer after 5yrs rental crisis. Crisis surcharge +$430/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$1,239/mo. Depletes W4.2 — runs out mid-February 2026, BEFORE the Iran war even begins.
tiers.savingsSource: Savings.com.au 2025: 50.2% of renters have less than $5,000; 27.4% have less than $1,000. Westpac 2025 median 30-34 = $1,104. After 5yrs of rental increases (20-30%+ in major cities), near-zero buffer is the norm for min wage renters.

tiers.name: Average Worker
tiers.notes: Median income $5,500/mo after tax. Debt: ~$488k. Liquid savings $6,500 — 5yr squeeze: 4.35% rates since May 2022, 7.8% peak inflation, mortgage repayments up 10% of income (RBA 2025). Westpac median 35-44 = $811 cash; average skewed by offset accounts. Crisis surcharge +$700/mo. Depletes W7.5 — exhausted before Iran war begins.
tiers.savingsSource: Westpac 2025: median 35-44 liquid cash = $811. Mortgage holder average $38,692 (Money.com.au 2025) but this includes offset/redraw — not freely accessible. RBA: mortgagors drew down savings buffers 2022-2025 under 4.35% rate pressure. Accessible cash estimate: $5-8k.

tiers.name: Middle Income
tiers.notes: $8,200/mo after tax. Debt: ~$1.05M. Liquid savings $18,000 — down from pre-pandemic buffer; debt service $8,875/mo exceeded income since 2022 rate rises, accelerating savings drawdown. Crisis surcharge +$900/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$7,598/mo. Depletes W10 — just after war start.
tiers.savingsSource: ABS 2021-22 distribution; Money.com.au 2025 mortgage holder average $38,692. Adjusted down ~50% for 2022-2025 drawdown: high debt service at 4.35% on $1M+ mortgage consumed cash reserves. Dual income helped maintain some buffer vs lower brackets.

tiers.name: High Income
tiers.notes: $13,000/mo after tax. Debt: ~$1.18M. Liquid savings $42,000 — down from higher pre-2022 buffer; $10,553/mo debt service consumed cash during 2022-2025 high-rate period. Asset-rich but cash-constrained (wealth is in property equity, not liquid). Crisis surcharge +$1,200/mo. Depletes W22.7 — survives longer but does not make it to W26.
tiers.savingsSource: Money.com.au 2025: mortgage holders average $38,692. High income bracket adjusted upward but constrained by $10,553/mo debt service on investment property ($1.18M). Asset-rich, cash-constrained. Some recovery after 2025 RBA rate cuts. Estimate: $40-50k accessible cash.

tiers.name: Retiree (Super)
tiers.notes: Super drawdown $3,800/mo + partial pension $1,100/mo = $4,900. Owns home. Debt: $32k (residual mortgage $22k nearly paid off + credit card $7k + BNPL $3k) → debt service $450/mo. 15% of 65+ carry housing debt; 39% carry credit card balance (Morningstar/NAB/RBA 2024-25). Crisis surcharge +$520/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$330/mo (debt service). Crisis total: -$850/mo. Still most resilient demographic — survives W26 with $33,860.
tiers.savingsSource: Morningstar Nov 2025: 15% of 65+ carry housing debt (double 1990 rate). Jacaranda/NAB 2024: 9% have active mortgage; 39% of 65+ carry outstanding credit card balance (highest of any age group). RBA: avg credit card balance $3,300. HILDA: 55+ household debt quadrupled 20yrs to $242k. Revised from $0 to $32k: residual mortgage $22k + credit card $7k + BNPL/personal $3k.

government.note: Excise cut expires Jun 30. Additional fiscal costs accelerate as price-shock tiers rise. At Crisis (12%) price-shock tier: government faces $5.5bn/month additional intervention cost.

cpiAlertTiers.label: Alert
cpiAlertTiers.description: Gov monitoring, strategic reserve monitoring activates

cpiAlertTiers.label: Warning
cpiAlertTiers.description: Reserve release triggers, national cabinet convenes

cpiAlertTiers.label: Crisis
cpiAlertTiers.description: Widescale rationing, price controls, compulsory acquisition, irreversible economic damage

inflation.note: 4-tier Price Shock Alert Framework. Regional price increase (9.8%) already in Emergency tier. NT Regional (14.2%) already in Crisis. National metro price increase (7.2%) in Warning. Peak expected May 2026. Food/fertiliser lag effect means worst still ahead — peak June-July 2026.

weeklyItems.label: LPG / Autogas
weeklyItems.basis: Crude-linked. Brent: pre-war +47.8% (OPEC discipline + demand recovery), war -1.8% (Hormuz). LPG at 65% crude sensitivity.

weeklyItems.label: Domestic airfares
weeklyItems.basis: Jet fuel (HO): pre-war +66.5% then war -0.7% (AUD-adj). Fuel is 32% of airline cost. War adds ~8% capacity premium (routes cut, demand shift).

weeklyItems.label: Sea freight
weeklyItems.basis: Pre-war: modest crude increase. War: Cape of Good Hope rerouting adds 14+ days and VLCC war risk premium. Hormuz closure is the primary driver here.

weeklyItems.label: Diesel (pump)
weeklyItems.basis: Crude oil: pre-war +47.8% then war -1.8%. Pump price: pre-war +40% (tensions building), war +-0% (Hormuz closure + Cape rerouting).

weeklyItems.label: Petrol (ULP)
weeklyItems.basis: RBOB futures: pre-war +57.0% then war +3.1% (AUD-adj). Pump: pre-war ~31%, war ~2%. Excise cut partially offset war increase.

weeklyItems.label: Fresh tomatoes
weeklyItems.basis: Cold chain diesel 35% + farm diesel 20% + freight 15%. Pre-war energy cost already rising. War-period adds Cape freight premium and farm input costs.

weeklyItems.label: Fresh lettuce
weeklyItems.basis: Cold chain diesel + farm energy + distribution. Similar to tomatoes. Pre-war cost base was already rising on diesel/energy pressures.

weeklyItems.label: Courier freight
weeklyItems.basis: Diesel pump +26.1% × 38% road freight cost share. Split mirrors diesel: pre-war tensions 15%, war -0%.

weeklyItems.label: Packaged groceries
weeklyItems.basis: Wheat +8.2% + crude packaging + freight +9.9%. Pre-war grain + energy rises dominate; war adds freight and packaging cost.

weeklyItems.label: Bread
weeklyItems.basis: Wheat: pre-war +15.9% (Ukraine war → Black Sea disruption, El Niño crop damage), war adds only -11.1% more. Bread increase is ~90% pre-war agricultural factors.

weeklyItems.label: Chicken
weeklyItems.basis: Grain feed: soy +9.7% (pre-war South American harvest), corn -3.7%. Grain moves were largely pre-war; war adds little to feed costs.

weeklyItems.label: Dairy / Milk
weeklyItems.basis: Feed grain (corn -3.7% YTD mostly pre-war) + refrigeration energy + freight. Modest increase — renewables buffer electricity, AUD partial offset.

fuelPrices.petrol: Capitals ~230-250c ULP avg (Sydney/Melbourne ~.40; Brisbane cheapest 219.5c; Perth cheapest 205.3c) — stabilising in major cities per ibtimes April 6

dbState.supply_review_json: {"title": "AustraFlow Prime Supply Review", "updatedAt": "2026-04-12T07:00:00Z", "summary": "AustraFlow Prime assessment for Apr 12 2026. Diesel reserves at 29 days \u2014 holding at critical threshold. Key development: servo outage pattern is shifting \u2014 diesel-only stations fell from 88 to 70 nationally, but petrol shortages are now emerging as the primary shortage type. This reflects the cargo arrival pattern: Largo Eagle docked Apr 10 (160 ML diesel), Nord Ventura arriving Port of Melbourne Apr 12 (310 ML). dbState.markets_review_json: {"title": "Hyperinflation Risk Assessment", "updatedAt": "2026-04-12T07:00:00Z", "summary": "Australia is in an EMERGENCY inflation phase \u2014 supply-shock driven, not monetary hyperinflation. The reduction reflects softening commodity markets, not actual retail price relief which lags by weeks. W29 weekly item series shows trajectory DECELERATING from W27 peak but levels remain emergency-high: diesel +44.9%, LPG +25.4%, domestic airfares +27.6%, sea freight +32.8% vs pre-war W1. The food second wave is the next pressure point: Coles/Woolworths suppliers pushing increases, grain producers warning +20% by Anzac Day. lastReport.summary: BLOCKADE ORDERED: Trump ordered naval blockade Iranian ports effective Apr 13 1400GMT; Islamabad talks collapsed; Brent back above US/bbl; Australia not joining blockade, pursuing SE Asia alternatives

feed.title: Fran Feed Review

topEvents.label: Geopolitical Tension
topEvents.headline: Trump orders naval blockade of Strait of Hormuz from April 13 at 1400GMT — ceasefire talks collapse; Iran submitted maximalist 10-point counterproposal

topEvents.label: Geopolitical Tension
topEvents.headline: CENTCOM confirms blockade enforcement from April 13 1400GMT — will NOT impede vessels transiting to/from non-Iranian ports; Iranian negotiator says 'we will not bow to any threats'

topEvents.label: Fuel Supply
topEvents.headline: Trump says US will start blockading Strait of Hormuz from April 13 — intercept ships paying Iran's crossing fee; WTI jumps 8% to /bbl, Brent to /bbl

topEvents.label: Fuel Supply
topEvents.headline: South Korea close to securing Kazakhstan crude — diversification from Hormuz feedstock

topEvents.label: Australian Exposure
topEvents.headline: Australia has not been asked to participate in US Hormuz blockade: Albanese. PM Albanese visits Brunei and Malaysia this week to shore up alternative fuel supply; key suppliers promising replacement from Africa/Americas

topEvents.label: Australian Exposure
topEvents.headline: Bowen: no shipment cancellations, contracted deliveries secured well into May, 4.1B litres locked in

topEvents.label: Shipping Routes
topEvents.headline: Reopening Strait of Hormuz insufficient to fix shipping woes: ~400 loaded oil tankers stuck in Gulf waiting to exit; clearance to July if fully reopened today

topEvents.label: Shipping Routes
topEvents.headline: Two oil supertankers execute U-turns in Strait of Hormuz after Islamabad talks collapse — shipping signal deteriorating

domainImpact.geopolitical: Conflict escalation directly drives the fuel supply squeeze. Each new strike on Gulf energy infrastructure or chokepoint control move extends the timeline before normal supply can resume. For Australians, the geopolitical signal is the upstream cause of higher pump prices, servo outages, and rising grocery costs.
domainImpact.fuel: Australia imports over 90% of its refined fuel from Asian refineries — primarily Singapore, South Korea, and Japan. Refinery output cuts, force majeure declarations, and export bans in those countries directly reduce the volume of diesel, petrol, and jet fuel arriving at Australian import terminals within 2–4 weeks.
domainImpact.shipping: Every tanker rerouted around the Cape of Good Hope adds 14+ days to delivery time and significantly raises freight cost. Fewer ships arriving on schedule means terminal drawdowns accelerate and servo outages spread from regional to suburban areas. Hormuz status is the single most important variable in the Australian supply outlook.
domainImpact.food: Fuel cost passes through to food via three channels: freight surcharges on fresh produce, fertiliser cost increases (Gulf urea exports are disrupted), and farm input costs rising for diesel-dependent agricultural machinery. The lag is typically 4–8 weeks from a fuel shock to visible grocery price increases.
domainImpact.australia: Domestic signals show how the global supply crunch is landing at street level. Servo outage counts, reserve levels, government policy actions, and regional price data are the leading indicators of whether the supply buffer is holding or deteriorating ahead of the mid-April critical threshold.

feed.routeSummary: Hormuz OPEN · Bab el-Mandeb THREATENED · Malacca OPEN
feed.supplyCeiling: Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026

australianImpact.chokepoints: Hormuz OPEN · Bab el-Mandeb THREATENED · Malacca OPEN
australianImpact.atPump: Diesel near or above A$3/L across most regions; some brief dips below A$2 for unleaded after excise cut. Regional stations — particularly in NSW, QLD, WA — intermittently dry for diesel.
australianImpact.groceries: Freight surcharges are feeding into fresh produce (tomatoes +65%, lettuce +55%), bread (+30%), and dairy. Transport cost increases embed in anything shipped by road or air. The full grocery impact peaks June–July 2026 as the fertiliser and farming input cascade works through.
australianImpact.travel: Domestic airfares up ~49% from pre-war baseline. Jet fuel constraints mean thin margins for regional routes. Easter travel is completing without cancellations, but further escalation would pressure route availability.
australianImpact.household: Estimated 3–5% monthly spending increase across transport, food, and utilities for the average Australian household. Low-income and regional households are hardest hit — diesel dependency for heating, farming, and long-distance commuting leaves no easy substitution.

feed.summary: 1255 events from 206 sources in 7 days. Dominated by Geopolitical Tension (650 events). Reserves: Diesel 29d · Petrol 39d · Jet fuel 30d. 269 stations dry. Routes: Hormuz OPEN · Bab el-Mandeb THREATENED · Malacca OPEN.
feed.mostLikelyCourse: Feed dominated by geopolitical signals. Supply under pressure but not in freefall. Critical test: Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026 supply ceiling — replacement cargoes must land before reserves breach the rationing trigger.
feed.mostDangerousCourse: Step-change escalation: US strikes on Iranian energy sites, Bab el-Mandeb closed alongside Hormuz, or Asian refinery force majeure. Any of these compresses the supply window and pushes servo outages into metro areas.
bullets: Diesel at 29 days — at or near the rationing threshold.
269 stations dry nationally — concentrated in regional NSW, QLD, WA.
April 6 Hormuz ultimatum — next critical decision point.
Grocery peak June–July 2026 — 4–8 week lag behind fuel. Worst still ahead.

supply.title: AustraFlow Prime — Supply Assessment
supply.summary: Diesel 29d (19d above 10d rationing trigger). Petrol 39d. Jet 30d. 269 stations dry (increasing). 5 vessels en route via Cape of Good Hope. Asian refineries 66% capacity. Domestic refining covers ~20% of demand (Lytton + Geelong). 80% import-dependent on foreign-flagged tankers with no obligation to prioritise Australia. Critical date: Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026.
supply.mostLikelyCourse: 5 cargoes land before Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026. Diesel holds above 10d. Regional servos restock as distribution catches up. Lytton and Geelong maintain output. Stressed but no rationing.
supply.scenarioB: Some cargoes land, others divert to higher bidders. Metro stabilises. Regional stays short — last-mile tanker frequency reduced. NT, remote WA, Cape York most exposed. Mining operations on emergency fuel management. National aggregate looks adequate but local distribution fails.
supply.mostDangerousCourse: Cargoes slip or divert. Diesel breaches 10d — rationing triggered. Southkorea output drops. Servo outages spread from regional to suburban. Cascade: diesel tightens → road tanker frequency drops → regional depots empty → mine sites on emergency power → food/medicine deliveries to remote communities delayed.
bullets: Diesel 29d — 19d above rationing trigger.
Asian refineries 66% — Southkorea at 55%.
269 stations dry — concentrated regional NSW, QLD, WA.

Critical date: Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026. No levers left if cargoes miss.
supply.confidenceReason: Vessel tracking confirmed. Asian refinery data estimated. Domestic distribution visibility limited to servo outage counts.

markets.title: Hyperinflation Risk Assessment
markets.summary: Australia is in a warning-phase inflation event driven by supply-side commodity pass-through, not classical hyperinflation. Live model: Metro 19.5%, Regional 21.3% — decelerating. Top movers: Diesel (pump) (+45.9%), Petrol (ULP) (+34.8%), Sea freight (+33.9%). 0 states CRISIS, 1 EMERGENCY. Not demand-pull — war-driven commodity pass-through.
markets.mostLikelyCourse: Prices plateau at current emergency levels through mid-April as incoming Cape-rerouted cargoes land (Largo Eagle Apr 10, Nord Ventura Apr 12, BP Thames Cargo Apr 15). Diesel pump holds 310-330 cpp nationally. Grocery index rises +8-12% more through May as supplier increases hit shelves. Metro CPI settles 11-14% range. Islamabad talks remain the critical near-term variable — any positive outcome eases freight/shipping costs; failure extends plateau.
markets.scenarioB: Partial stabilisation. 1979 precedent — premature easing led to reinflation. Conflict pauses, Hormuz partially reopens under escort. Metro retreats to 14–16% but stalls. Excise extended at reduced rate. Not the sharpest peak but the slowest recovery.
markets.mostDangerousCourse: Ground war extends beyond ceasefire window. Hormuz de facto closure extends into May. Diesel rationing May 5 triggers scarcity premium compounding baseline. Food second wave (fertiliser/transport) hits May-June simultaneously. Diesel could reach 350-380 cpp. Equivalent to 1970s oil shock for affected households.
bullets: Live Price Shock Monitor anchor: Metro 19.5% → peak 22.4%. Regional 21.3% → 24.5%.
Current phase by shared threshold map: WARNING.
0 states CRISIS, 1 EMERGENCY.
AUD below 0.60 accelerates pass-through. Excise expiry June adds ~15 cpl.
Fair Work Q3 wage decision — indexation trigger.
markets.confidenceReason: Brent/WTI/AUDUSD from live Yahoo Finance (Apr 12). Pump prices from Guardian/AIP confirmed Apr 9 — slightly stale for today. Item series from model. Confidence medium because the ceasefire outcome (Islamabad talks) is the decisive near-term variable and cannot be modelled from market data alone.

households.title: Household Stress Assessment
households.summary: 0 of 8 households in deficit. 1 more within $100 of tipping. The war-driven crisis adds avg $238/mo per household (fuel +28.2%, groceries +8.7%), but it is not the primary threat. Debt service is. Average Worker has a TDSR of 44% — above the 40% danger line. Remove non-mortgage debt and every tier survives.
households.mostLikelyCourse: Average Worker enters discretionary compression — streaming, dining, non-essential driving cut first. Average Worker hold but have no buffer left. Excise relief expires June 30. If extended, all tiers stabilise. If not, fuel adds ~$65/mo and tips the razor-thin tiers into deficit.
households.scenarioB: Excise extended at 50%, RBA holds. Average Worker burn rate drops to near zero. Average Worker return to safe surplus. Food stays elevated through Q3 2026. Households already in compression don't restore spending for 3–6 months — retail and hospitality feel the drag. CC balances accumulated during the deficit take 6–12 months to clear.
households.mostDangerousCourse: Excise lapses June 30. Grocery peak hits simultaneously. Average Worker burn rate doubles. Average Worker tip into deficit. Households fund the gap with credit cards at 20% pa — higher balance, higher minimum, higher burn, more CC. If RBA hikes +25bp on top: +$121/mo for Average Worker. That is the scenario that triggers mortgage arrears.
bullets: Average Worker TDSR 44% — above 40% danger line.
Average Worker within $100 of deficit — one shock tips them.
Remove debt: all 8 tiers survive. Debt is the amplifier.
Excise expiry June 30 — +$65/mo if not extended.
households.confidenceReason: Balance sheets sourced from ABS HES 6530.0, ATO 2025-26, RBA mortgage calc. Crisis surcharge live from Markets commodity data.

domino.title: Domino Cascade — Review
domino.summary: Base Case — Stalemate at 40%. Escalation 35%. Resolution 25%. The two leading scenarios are close — one event shifts the balance. Troop phase (DEPLOYMENT) is the swing variable. Ground ops would push escalation above base and lock in a 6+ month conflict: GDP -5.0%, food +40%, recovery 12–24 months.
domino.mostLikelyCourse: Base Case — Stalemate (40%) holds. Aerial conflict only. Supply tightens to Extended to May 2026 — 53 ships with 3.7B litres confirmed; Bowen confirms no cancellations; original mid-April supply ceiling now pushed out — source: Bowen/AAP 06/04/2026 then stabilises. Food peaks +20% May–June. GDP -2.1%. Recovery 3–6 months.
domino.mostDangerousCourse: Escalation (35%). Ground invasion or major Gulf strike. Hormuz closed 6–18 months. Rationing active. Food +40%. Recovery 12–24 months.
bullets: Base 40% vs Escalation 35% — near tipping point.
Resolution 25% — erodes daily without diplomatic progress.
Troop phase is the swing — ground ops shifts escalation +15–20pp.

troops.title: J2 — Friendly Disposition & Iran TCVs
troops.summary: BATTLESPACE: DEPLOYMENT. Friendly 53,275 pers (32 units) vs Iran 60,000 pers. Force ratio 0.9:1. IRAN TCVs — Troops: 50,000 ground (3 divs + 2 reserve), 5,000 IRGC Navy (Hormuz denial), 2,000 Quds Force (proxy ops). Capabilities: Hormuz mine/ASM barrier, Shaheed drone swarms, ballistic missile TELs (Emad, Sejjil), proxy network across 4 theatres. Vulnerabilities: no credible air defence vs F-35, logistics on single western corridor, proxy C2 fragile under comms disruption.
troops.mostLikelyCourse: Iran maintains Hormuz denial posture. IRGC Navy holds mine barrier and fast attack screen. No ground commitment beyond border provinces. Drone/missile strikes at current tempo. Proxies sustain harassment, no escalation. Friendly coalition holds air superiority and naval escort. DEPLOYMENT holds.
troops.scenarioB: Iran partially reopens Hormuz under pressure — escort corridor established but mine threat remains. Ground divisions hold defensive. Missile strikes reduced. Proxy tempo decreases if Quds Force C2 disrupted. Friendly posture shifts from strike to containment.
troops.mostDangerousCourse: Iran commits ground forces offensively — border divisions push into Iraq or threaten Gulf states. Reserve divisions deploy forward (late April). IRGC escalates to full Hormuz + Bab el-Mandeb closure via Houthis. Ballistic salvo against Gulf infrastructure. Friendly ground commitment required — 82nd Airborne, 101st activate. Conflict extends 12–24 months.
bullets: Iran ground: 3 divs deployed, 2 reserve en route. Movement = escalation.
IRGC Navy: mine-laying in Hormuz. New mining triggers naval response.
Ballistic TELs: dispersal patterns indicate launch readiness.
Quds Force proxy tempo: Houthis (Red Sea), Hezbollah (north), Iraqi militia.
Force ratio 0.9:1 — friendly disadvantaged ground, dominant air/naval.
troops.confidenceReason: OSINT only. Classified deployments not visible. Iran ground numbers estimated from imagery.

troopDeployments.phaseReason: Apr 12 UPDATE — MATERIAL DETERIORATION. Islamabad talks first phase concluded Apr 11; Iran REJECTED US 45-day ceasefire proposal and submitted its own 10-point counterproposal (solution to ALL regional conflicts, full sanctions lift, reconstruction, Hormuz protocol). Iran Qalibaf: bilateral ceasefire or negotiations is unreasonable. Ceasefire framework FRACTURING. Israel continues Lebanon strikes (IDF Zamir: will continue). IDF F-35I shot down Iranian Yak-130 over Tehran (first F-35 air-to-air kill). Two US destroyers crossed Hormuz Apr 11 for mine clearance. Hormuz traffic less than 10 percent normal. Phase remains DEPLOYMENT but risk of re-escalation INCREASING. Islamabad next round expected Apr 11-12 — outcome will determine if phase escalates to GROUND OPS.
troopDeployments.invasionIndicatorReason: UNCHANGED Apr 12 — Islamabad talks outcome PENDING. Iran rejected US proposal, submitted maximalist 10-point counterproposal. Ceasefire framework fracturing — Israel continuing Lebanon strikes. IDF F-35I air-to-air kill over Tehran. Ground invasion threshold NOT crossed while talks continue, but next 24-48h CRITICAL. Invasion score HOLDING at 61 — key triggers: (1) Islamabad collapse, (2) Iranian strike on US/coalition asset, (3) Lebanon ceasefire collapse, (4) next round of Islamabad fails to narrow gap. Hormuz normalisation severely constrained regardless of diplomacy outcome. Iran mobilising 1M fighters incl. child recruits 12+ (ongoing). Russia providing Iran satellite intel (ISW Apr 7). IDF planning 3 more weeks of operations.
troopDeployments.supplyChainImplication: Apr 12 UPDATE — CEASEFIRE FRAMEWORK FRACTURING. Islamabad first phase concluded Apr 11: Iran rejected US 45-day proposal, submitted 10-point counterproposal. Hormuz traffic less than 10 percent normal despite ceasefire — Iran reimposed 15 ships/day cap. Only a few vessels crossed in first days. Ceasefire did NOT normalise shipping. Next 24-48h critical for supply chain outlook: if Islamabad next round fails to narrow gap, Hormuz cap could tighten further and full re-escalation risk returns. Australia negotiating directly with Asian fuel suppliers (ABC confirmed Apr 9) — government-level fuel security concern confirmed. Diesel normalisation delayed. Ground war scenario keeps Hormuz restricted or closed indefinitely.

decisionTriggers: Iranian strike on US asset requiring ground response
IDF request for US infantry support

indicators.name: Ground Force Positioning
indicators.evidence: IDF struck Tehran airports overnight (Apr 6). IDF/Mossad confirmed operating inside Iran. 3 Iranian divisions mobilised to western border. 2 reserve divisions en route (ETA late April). MAJOR: Iran mobilising 1 million fighters including child recruits age 12+ (HRW, India Today, Apr 7). Human chains and mass volunteer force being prepared as last-line defence. Ground force positioning accelerating sharply.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 70 when forward staging areas detected or divisions move to assembly points within 100km of border.

indicators.name: Logistics & Sustainment
indicators.evidence: USS Bush CSG deploying. 101st Airborne en route late April. Field hospitals not yet deployed. Logistics window opening mid-April per baseline — no forward staging areas established yet, but convergence of lift capability.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 60 when field hospitals, bridging equipment, or fuel depots detected near forward positions.

indicators.name: SEAD/DEAD Campaign
indicators.evidence: IDF struck Asaluyeh petrochemical complex (85% of Iran petrochemical exports, Apr 6). IDF struck Iranian military aircraft and helicopters in three Tehran airports overnight (Apr 6). IDF planning 3 more weeks of systematic operations to degrade Iran defence industry (Times of Israel). Systematic SEAD/prep campaign confirmed — strikes are retaliatory AND preparatory for potential ground phase.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 75 when systematic suppression of all Iranian IADS detected — this precedes any ground advance by 48-72 hours.

indicators.name: Amphibious / Sealift
indicators.evidence: USS Tripoli (LHA-7) with 31st MEU in Gulf of Oman. USS Eisenhower in Arabian Sea. USS Bush CSG deploying (confirmed, Atlantic Council/USNI). France scaling to 12 ships in wider Middle East (NYT, Apr 6). Netherlands HNLMS Evertsen extended to early May (Navy Lookout). 35-nation Hormuz Coalition escort active. Amphibious capability present — HMS Canberra en route late April. HMS Dragon docked Eastern Med with minor technical issue (Sky News/Daily Mail, Apr 7-8).
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 70 when MEU shifts to assault posture or additional amphibious ships surge to theatre.

indicators.name: SOF / Intelligence Tempo
indicators.evidence: IDF struck Tehran airports (Apr 6). IDF/Mossad confirmed operating inside Iran. US SOF (Rangers, SEALs) at undisclosed Middle East locations. ADF SAS at Al Minhad. SOF tempo elevated — consistent with target preparation phase.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 80 when SOF shift from reconnaissance to target preparation (bridge seizure, airfield marking).

indicators.name: Diplomatic Off-Ramp
indicators.evidence: Apr 8: 2-week ceasefire agreed — Trump suspended strikes, Iran reopened Hormuz for two weeks, talks to resume in Islamabad Apr 11. OFF-RAMP TEMPORARILY OPENED. However: Iran still mobilising 1 million fighters (incl. child recruits 12+). Russia providing Iran satellite imagery of US/Gulf assets (ISW Apr 7). IDF planning 3 more weeks post-ceasefire. Back-channels active but narrow. Ceasefire fragile — 2 weeks to reach settlement or risk full re-escalation. UN Security Council has not passed binding ceasefire. Score stabilised — ceasefire changed trajectory but has not resolved underlying conflict.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 80 when all back-channels confirmed closed and UN Security Council fails to pass ceasefire resolution.

indicators.name: Reserve / Conscript Call-up
indicators.evidence: No US/UK/Australia reserve call-up. Iran mobilising 1 million fighters including child recruits age 12+ (HRW, India Today, Apr 7). IRGC child recruitment campaign underway. Human chains being planned as last-line defence. 2 IRGC reserve divisions en route (ETA late April). This is the largest mobilisation signal from Iran to date.
indicators.threshold: Score reaches 70 when any coalition nation activates reserve forces or extends deployments beyond normal rotation.

equations: Scenario severity λ: resolution = 0, base = 1/3, escalation = 1

scenarios.name: Base Case — Stalemate
scenarios.description: Neither side achieves decisive advantage. US/Iran settle into attritional standoff without ground invasion. Impact remains channeled through fuel prices and shipping disruption only.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility shifts into managed constraint: freight first, patchy regional fuel access, reduced discretionary travel, and persistent aviation disruption.
consequences: Freight and essential services are prioritised before private mobility.
Regional shortages stay uneven even if metro supply holds.
Domestic aviation remains reduced and expensive for longer.
Households feel availability and price pain before formal controls.

scenarios.name: Escalation — Ground War
scenarios.description: US deploys ground forces. Iran closes Hormuz. Full fuel shock, travel restrictions cascade into productivity and food. Food prices peak at +40% with permanent structural change.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility risk becomes systemic. Freight gets protected, aviation shrinks, regional access degrades, and rationing becomes a live policy option.
consequences: Freight corridors, agriculture, emergency services and buses get priority access.
Domestic aviation and airfreight contract quickly if jet fuel tightens.
Regional household fuel access becomes patchy before metro rationing.
Formal allocation and movement controls become plausible if shortages deepen.

scenarios.name: Resolution — Ceasefire
scenarios.description: Trump-Khamenei back-channel holds. Temporary ceasefire agreed. Fuel prices ease within weeks. Food impact stays below +10%.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility remains stressed but functional. Expect delays, higher prices, and selective conservation rather than formal restrictions.
consequences: Domestic flights remain expensive and patchy before recovering.
Voluntary fuel conservation is more likely than rationing.
Regional freight delays ease first if routes reopen.

southKorea.name: South Korea
southKorea.exportNote: Exports capped at 2025 monthly average. SK Energy, GS Caltex, S-Oil all complying.
southKorea.australiaRelevance: PRIMARY — largest single source of Australian diesel imports (~120,000 b/d pre-war)
southKorea.feedstockSource: Middle East crude (~75%) — directly affected by Hormuz closure
southKorea.keyRisk: Feedstock starvation. Iranian/Iraqi crude transit via Hormuz now blocked. Korean refiners drawing on strategic reserves and US spot cargoes at premium.

facilities.name: SK Energy Ulsan
facilities.note: Largest single refinery. Running 2 of 3 CDUs.

facilities.name: GS Caltex Yeosu
facilities.note: Diesel-heavy output. Export allocation to Australia maintained but capped.

facilities.name: S-Oil Onsan
facilities.note: Saudi Aramco JV — feedstock priority from Saudi.

crudeProfile.canProcessUSCrude: Partial — can blend WTI up to 30%
crudeProfile.vulnerability: HIGH — 75% of crude diet transits Hormuz. Complex refineries can adapt but need sour feedstock.

singapore.name: Singapore
singapore.exportNote: No formal export restrictions. Market-driven allocation — highest bidder wins.
singapore.australiaRelevance: CRITICAL — Singapore complex margin is the pricing reference for all Australian fuel imports
singapore.feedstockSource: Middle East (~60%), West Africa, US — diversified but Hormuz exposure significant
singapore.keyRisk: Margin squeeze. Singapore crack spreads elevated but feedstock cost rising faster. Export incentive may erode.

facilities.name: Shell Pulau Bukom
facilities.note: Largest Singapore refinery. Partial turnaround deferred.

facilities.name: ExxonMobil Jurong
facilities.note: Running above Shell due to US crude access.

crudeProfile.alternativeSources: US WTI, Nigerian Bonny Light, Russian Urals
crudeProfile.vulnerability: MEDIUM — most flexible refineries in region. Already taking US spot cargoes.

japan.name: Japan
japan.exportNote: Domestic priority. Limited export surplus. 254 days strategic reserves — most resilient in region.
japan.australiaRelevance: SECONDARY — Japan exports some jet fuel and naphtha to Australia but prioritises domestic
japan.feedstockSource: Middle East (~80%) — highest Hormuz exposure. Offset by massive 254-day strategic reserve.
japan.keyRisk: Low — Japan can sustain domestic supply for months from reserves. Export availability minimal.

facilities.name: ENEOS Negishi
facilities.note: Largest. Running near capacity on strategic reserve crude.

crudeProfile.alternativeSources: 254-day strategic reserve is the primary buffer. Limited crude switching.
crudeProfile.vulnerability: HIGH exposure but LOW urgency — massive strategic reserve buys time.

india.name: India
india.exportNote: EU-bound diesel volumes freed by Russian crude restrictions now available for Australia. Potential new supply source.
india.australiaRelevance: EMERGING — India redirecting diesel that was going to Europe. First AU-bound cargo (Indian Resolve) in transit.
india.feedstockSource: Russia (~40%), Middle East (~35%), Africa — least Hormuz-exposed major refiner
india.keyRisk: Political — Indian government may restrict exports if domestic prices rise. Quality compatibility with AU diesel spec needs verification.

facilities.name: Jamnagar (Reliance)
facilities.note: World largest. Russian crude access gives cost advantage.

facilities.name: Paradip (IOCL)
facilities.note: East coast — shorter route to Australia.

crudeProfile.primaryGrade: Russian Urals + Arab Light + Basra Heavy
crudeProfile.alternativeSources: Can process any crude on earth. Least constrained.
crudeProfile.vulnerability: LOW — Russian crude access via direct route gives cost + supply advantage.

malaysia.name: Malaysia
malaysia.exportNote: Domestic prioritisation policy active. Warning to export partners issued March 15.
malaysia.australiaRelevance: SIGNIFICANT — Malaysia supplies ~40% of Australia crude feedstock + refined jet fuel
malaysia.feedstockSource: Domestic + Middle East — moderate Hormuz exposure
malaysia.keyRisk: Domestic prioritisation could cut Australian crude supply. Petronas has discretion to redirect.

facilities.name: Petronas RAPID Pengerang
facilities.note: Newest. Export-oriented but now domestic priority.

facilities.name: Petronas Melaka
facilities.note: Crude/feedstock for Lytton refinery sourced here.

crudeProfile.alternativeSources: Domestic Tapis/Miri crude reduces import dependency
crudeProfile.vulnerability: LOW — domestic crude production insulates from Hormuz. Key risk is domestic prioritisation policy.

china.name: China
china.exportNote: Full refined product export suspension in effect since March 8. Zero availability for Australia.
china.australiaRelevance: FORMER PRIMARY — China was dominant jet fuel supplier (~190,000 b/d). Now zero.
china.feedstockSource: Russia, Middle East, West Africa, Americas — diversified
china.keyRisk: Export suspension is the single largest supply shock to Australia. Jet fuel gap has no quick replacement.

crudeProfile.primaryGrade: Russian ESPO + Iranian Heavy + Venezuelan Merey + Arab Light
crudeProfile.primaryOrigin: Russia (~30%), Iran (~15%), Venezuela (~8%), Saudi Arabia (~15%), Angola, Brazil
crudeProfile.alternativeSources: Russian ESPO pipeline (no maritime risk), Iranian crude via ship-to-ship transfer (sanctions evasion), Venezuelan Merey heavy crude
crudeProfile.vulnerability: LOW for supply, HIGH for Australia — full export suspension means zero availability regardless of Chinese refinery health.
crudeProfile.iranVenezuelaNotes: China is the PRIMARY buyer of both Iranian and Venezuelan sanctioned crude. Pre-war: Iran exported ~1.5M bpd, of which ~1.2M bpd went to China via ship-to-ship transfers in Malaysian/Omani waters. Venezuela exported ~600k bpd, ~400k bpd to China (Sinopec, PetroChina). Both use dark fleet tankers with AIS transponders off. Chinese teapot refineries (Shandong province) are the main processors — they have the coking capacity for Venezuelan Merey (API 16, very heavy). This trade continues during the current conflict — China has not sanctioned Iran and continues purchasing. The volume has actually INCREASED since the war began as China stockpiles at discounted prices.

australia.name: Australia (Domestic)
australia.exportNote: Both refineries running at maximum. Gasoline-heavy output — does not solve diesel deficit.
australia.australiaRelevance: ONLY domestic source — covers ~20% of total demand but structurally mismatched (petrol not diesel)
australia.feedstockSource: Malaysia crude (~40%), US crude (~21%) — both supply chains under pressure
australia.keyRisk: Feedstock disruption from Malaysia or unplanned outage at either refinery would be catastrophic.

facilities.name: Ampol Lytton (Brisbane)
facilities.note: Maintenance deferred. Running above design for crisis.

facilities.name: Viva Geelong (Melbourne)
facilities.note: Normal operations. Fuel Security Services Payment active.

crudeProfile.alternativeSources: Nigerian Bonny Light (compatible but expensive). CANNOT process ME sour crude.
crudeProfile.vulnerability: CRITICAL structural mismatch — simple refineries (NCI 6.5-7.0) cannot desulfurise. Malaysia is irreplaceable feedstock. Even if Hormuz reopens, Australia cannot use ME crude directly.

hazards.impact: All AU-bound vessels rerouted via Cape of Good Hope. +14 days transit. +$1-2M per voyage.
Houthi attacks on commercial shipping. War risk insurance surcharge applied. Some vessels avoiding.
Overflow from Malacca as Hormuz-bypass traffic increases. Wait times 12-24 hours.
Autumn swell season. No cyclone activity. Minor speed reduction for Cape route vessels.
Fuel tanker berth competition with container ships. 24-48hr wait for discharge slot.
No disruption to TAS/VIC coastal fuel movements.
BP Thames Cargo expected mid-April. No berth congestion.

delayTable.cause: Cape of Good Hope reroute (Hormuz closed)
Lombok Strait routing + Port Botany queue
